title: Catalog Glossary
description: Plain-English definitions for terms and acronyms that appear across the catalog. Each entry has a one-line gloss in everyday language and an optional longer note. Pattern pages link these terms inline so a reader new to agent vocabulary does not have to leave the page.

terms.gloss: A model that takes text in and produces text out, trained on a lot of text. ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are LLMs.
Before answering a question, the system fetches relevant documents from a search index and includes them in the prompt, so the model has source material to read instead of guessing from memory.
A shared protocol so any tool written for one agent host (Claude Desktop, Cursor, your own CLI) can be used by any other host without rewriting the integration.
An old, fast keyword-matching scoring algorithm used by search engines. Good at exact-token matches like product codes and proper nouns; poor at semantic similarity.
A list of numbers that represents the meaning of a piece of text. Two pieces of text with similar meanings have similar embeddings, so 'nearest-neighbour' search finds semantically related content.
A graph of steps where each step points forward to its dependents and never loops back. Useful for parallelising independent steps in a plan.
A standard way of describing the shape of a JSON object — what fields it must have, what types they are. Lets a validator reject calls that don't match before they reach the executor.
The set of typed function-like tools an agent is allowed to call. The agent picks one per turn; the host validates the call against the palette's schema.
The infrastructure layer that hosts and executes tools — separate from the model itself. Tools live in the tool plane; the model only emits calls into it.
A hard cap on how many loop iterations or tool calls an agent may make before it must stop. Prevents runaway behaviour.
One unit of agent work, usually 'from a user message to the next user message' (including all tool calls in between). Memory and reasoning traces are often scoped to a single episode.
Replacing older parts of the conversation history with shorter summaries to free up context-window space while keeping the gist.
After an initial fast retriever returns ~100 candidates, a slower, more accurate model scores each one against the query and reorders them. Trades inference cost for sharper top-k.
Showing the model a handful of input/output examples in the prompt to teach it the format or task, instead of fine-tuning.
Asking the model to do a task with no examples, just an instruction. The opposite of few-shot.
Asking the model to write out intermediate reasoning steps before giving the final answer. Improves accuracy on multi-step problems but costs more tokens.
A routing layer that sends each request to the specialist sub-model best suited for it, instead of running every request through one giant model.
A point in the workflow where a human reviews or approves an agent's action before it executes.
An internal cache the LLM keeps for tokens it has already processed in a long context, so subsequent generation is faster. Prompt caching exposes this externally.
A vendor-side cache (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google all offer this) that lets you re-send the same long prompt prefix cheaply. Useful when the system prompt is huge but stable across calls.
A restricted environment where untrusted code or tool calls run with limited capabilities (no network, no host filesystem) so they cannot damage the host system.
A child agent invoked by a parent agent to handle a bounded sub-task. Has its own context, tools, and step budget; returns a single structured result to the parent.
The full sequence of an agent's thoughts, tool calls, observations, and intermediate state on one task — what the agent did, not just what it said in the end.
Anthropic's approach to alignment where the model is trained to follow an explicit list of principles ('the constitution') and to critique and revise its own outputs against them.
An auditable record of where an output came from: which tool calls produced it, which documents were retrieved, which model version was running.
A distributed-systems pattern where each step in a multi-step workflow is paired with a compensating action that can undo it if a later step fails.
A protection layer that stops calling a failing dependency after error rates exceed a threshold, then trial-calls again after a cooldown. Prevents retry storms and wasted cost.
A minimum number of independent agents (or replicas) that must agree before an action can proceed. Stops a single misbehaving agent from causing damage.
A web page that returns HTTP 200 (success) but actually shows a 'page not found' message. Looks alive to a HEAD probe; dead to a real reader.
The maximum number of tokens (roughly: words plus punctuation) a model can read in one call. Anything beyond it gets truncated or paged.
Continuing to train a base model on a smaller, more specific dataset so it specialises in a domain or task.
A training technique where humans rank pairs of model outputs and the model learns to prefer the preferred one. The dominant alignment recipe for chat models.
A portable binary format that runs sandboxed at near-native speed in many languages. Useful for executing third-party agent skills without trusting them with host privileges.
